fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Agreement on a class of figure shapes

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let \bar {s} be a displayed object in a cartesian fibration E over B. A pair of displayed morphisms { \bar {x} } \xrightarrow {{ f,g }}{ \bar {y} } \in E are said to agree on \bar {s}-figures when for any \bar {s}-figure { \bar {z} } \xrightarrow {{ h }}{ \bar {x} }, we have { \bar {z} } \xrightarrow {{ h;f = h;g }}{ \bar {y} }.

In a category E, a morphism { x } \xrightarrow {{ f }}{ y } can be thought of as a “figure” of shape x drawn in y. For instance, if x is the point (i.e. x= \mathbf {1} _{ E }) then a morphism x \to y is a “point” of the “space” y. We refer to x as the figure-shape in any such scenario. The perspective of morphisms as figures is developed in more detail by Lawvere and Schanuel.

It often happens that a useful class of figure shapes can be arranged into a set-indexed family { \mathopen {} \left ( u _{ i } \right ) \mathclose {}} _{ i \in I }; viewed from the perspective of the family fibration \boldsymbol { \mathcal {F}} _{ E }, this family is just a displayed object \bar {u} over I and then a figure shape “in” this family is given by any cartesian morphism \bar {z} \to \bar {u}. We will generalize this situation to the case of an arbitrary fibration, by constructing the full subfibration spanned by displayed objects equipped with a cartesian morphism into \bar {u} in the associated full subfibration below.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The full subfibration associated to a displayed object

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let E be a cartesian fibration over B; then any displayed object \bar {x} \in E _{ x } induces a full subfibration \mathbf {Full} { \mathopen {} \left ( \bar {u} \right ) \mathclose {}} \subseteq E spanned by displayed objects that are classified by \bar {u}, i.e. arise from \bar {u} by cartesian lift.

fr:ol.fr:li: An object of \mathbf {Full} { \mathopen {} \left ( \bar {u} \right ) \mathclose {}} _{ x } is specified by an object \bar {x} \in E _{ x } together with a cartesian morphism \bar {x} \to \bar {u}.
Given { x } \xrightarrow {{ f }}{ y } \in B, a morphism from \bar {x} \to \bar {u} to \bar {y} \to \bar {u} \in over f is given by any displayed morphism \bar {x} \xrightarrow [ f ]{} \bar {y}.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Figures and figure shapes in the full subfibration

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let E be a cartesian fibration and let \mathbf {Full} { \mathopen {} \left ( \bar {s} \right ) \mathclose {}} \subseteq E be the full subfibration determined by a displayed object \bar {s} \in E. We now develop the following vocabulary:

fr:ol.fr:li: We will refer to each object of \mathbf {Full} { \mathopen {} \left ( \bar {s} \right ) \mathclose {}} as a \bar {s}-figure shape.
A displayed morphism \bar {z} \to \bar {x} is called a \bar {s}-figure whenever \bar {z} \in \mathbf {Full} { \mathopen {} \left ( \bar {s} \right ) \mathclose {}}.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Agreement on a class of figure shapes

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let \bar {s} be a displayed object in a cartesian fibration E over B. A pair of displayed morphisms { \bar {x} } \xrightarrow {{ f,g }}{ \bar {y} } \in E are said to agree on \bar {s}-figures when for any \bar {s}-figure { \bar {z} } \xrightarrow {{ h }}{ \bar {x} }, we have { \bar {z} } \xrightarrow {{ h;f = h;g }}{ \bar {y} }.

Let E be an ordinary category. In general, to compare two morphisms { x } \xrightarrow {{ f,g }}{ y } in E, it is not enough to see if they agree on global points { 1 } \xrightarrow {{ u }}{ x }, because the behavior of f,g may differ only on generalized elements. In some cases, however, there is a family of objects { \mathopen {} \left ( s _{ i } \right ) \mathclose {}} _{ i \in I } \in E are together adequate for comparing morphisms of E in the sense of separating families below.

Given an ordinary category E, a set-indexed family { \mathopen {} \left ( s _{ i } \right ) \mathclose {}} _{ i \in I } of E-objects is called a small separating family for E when, assuming that for all i \in I and all { s_i } \xrightarrow {{ u }}{ x } we have u;f=u;g, we then have f=g.

The intuition of separating families is that to compare two morphisms { x } \xrightarrow {{ f,g }}{ y } \in E, it suffices to check that they behave the same on all s _{ i }-shaped figures when { \mathopen {} \left ( s _{ i } \right ) \mathclose {}} _{ i \in I } is a separating family for E.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Well-pointedness of the category of sets

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: In the category of sets, to compare two morphisms it is enough to check that they agree on global points. This means that the unary family { \mathopen {} \left \{ \mathbf {1} _{ \mathbf {Set} } \right \} \mathclose {}} is a separator for \mathbf {Set}, a property referred to more generally as well-pointedness.
We will now generalize the notion of separating family to the case of a cartesian fibration.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Agreement on a class of figure shapes

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let \bar {s} be a displayed object in a cartesian fibration E over B. A pair of displayed morphisms { \bar {x} } \xrightarrow {{ f,g }}{ \bar {y} } \in E are said to agree on \bar {s}-figures when for any \bar {s}-figure { \bar {z} } \xrightarrow {{ h }}{ \bar {x} }, we have { \bar {z} } \xrightarrow {{ h;f = h;g }}{ \bar {y} }.

Let E be a cartesian fibration over B such that B has binary products. A displayed object \bar {s} \in E _{ s } is said to be a small separator for E when any two vertical maps f,g: \bar {u} \xrightarrow [ 1 _{ x } ]{} \bar {v} \in E _{ x } are equal when they are agree on \bar {s}-figures.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Figures and figure shapes in the full subfibration

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let E be a cartesian fibration and let \mathbf {Full} { \mathopen {} \left ( \bar {s} \right ) \mathclose {}} \subseteq E be the full subfibration determined by a displayed object \bar {s} \in E. We now develop the following vocabulary:

fr:ol.fr:li: We will refer to each object of \mathbf {Full} { \mathopen {} \left ( \bar {s} \right ) \mathclose {}} as a \bar {s}-figure shape.
A displayed morphism \bar {z} \to \bar {x} is called a \bar {s}-figure whenever \bar {z} \in \mathbf {Full} { \mathopen {} \left ( \bar {s} \right ) \mathclose {}}.

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let E be a cartesian fibration over B such that B has binary products. A displayed object \bar {s} \in E _{ s } is said to be a small separator for E when any two vertical maps f,g: \bar {u} \xrightarrow [ 1 _{ x } ]{} \bar {v} \in E _{ x } are equal when they are agree on \bar {s}-figures.
